title: Oat biscuits, hobnob-style
subtitle: Crunchy oat-and-golden-syrup biscuits
excerpt: Crisp British oat biscuits in the hobnob style: rolled oats, plain flour, butter, brown sugar and golden syrup. Twenty biscuits from one tray, ready for tea in half an hour.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): documents oat-and-syrup biscuits across Yorkshire and the North as a household tea-tray standard. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: oat-based biscuits appear in the breakfast-cakes chapter. The hobnob shape (a thick crisp oat biscuit) is a modern commercial form synthesised here from the pre-1928 oat-biscuit family.

recipe.freezeNotes: Bake, cool, freeze in a tin for up to 3 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough holds in the fridge for 2 days; rest at room temperature 10 minutes before shaping.

recipeTools.notes: For melting the butter and syrup.

content.text: Hobnob-style oat biscuits are a melt-and-mix bake: butter, brown sugar and golden syrup are warmed together, poured into rolled oats and flour, and the mix is rolled into balls and pressed flat. The biscuits crisp as they cool, going from soft on the tray to snap-crisp on the rack over about ten minutes.
For a chocolate-coated version, dip the underside of each cooled biscuit in melted dark chocolate and set on baking paper until firm.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan and line two baking trays. Mix the {{rolled-oats}}, {{plain-flour}}, {{soft-brown-sugar}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a wide bowl. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{golden-syrup}} together in a small pan over a low heat. Pour the warm liquid into the dry mix and stir until evenly combined; the mix should clump together when pressed in your palm. Roll into 20 balls (about 30 g each), place on the trays with 4 cm between, and flatten each to a 5 mm disc with the palm of your hand.

Bake for 12–15 minutes; the biscuits should be deep gold across the top and spread a little. They will be soft when they come out — that is correct. Cool on the trays for 5 minutes (they crisp as they cool), then transfer to a rack.
